name: tRPC
what_it_does: End-to-end type-safe API framework for TypeScript monorepos that generates fully-typed React Query hooks from server router definitions without a separate schema language.
use_cases: Generating a typesafe fullstack API where TypeScript itself is the schema — no Zod/OpenAPI schema needed separately
Scaffolding tRPC routers with query and mutation procedures that auto-produce typed client hooks
Integrating tRPC with Next.js App Router or Pages Router using the Next.js adapter
Using input validation via Zod schemas inside procedure .input() calls to validate agent-generated payloads at runtime
Building monorepo APIs where the server package exports the AppRouter type consumed by client packages for zero-drift type safety
not_for: Public APIs consumed by third-party clients in non-TypeScript environments — tRPC's type safety breaks at language boundaries
Teams that need a REST or GraphQL API surface for external consumers
Projects where the frontend and backend are in separate repos without shared TypeScript types
best_when: Building TypeScript fullstack monorepos (especially Next.js) where eliminating the client/server contract drift is the primary goal.
avoid_when: The API must be consumed by non-TypeScript clients or the team needs a public, documented REST/GraphQL interface.

auth.notes: Auth is handled via the context function; tRPC itself is auth-agnostic — agents must wire in NextAuth, Lucia, or custom JWT validation in createContext.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: tRPC surfaces Zod validation errors as structured TRPCError with BAD_REQUEST code; TypeScript compile errors catch contract mismatches before runtime.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Queries are idempotent by design; mutations require application-level idempotency keys — tRPC does not enforce this.
known_agent_gotchas: The AppRouter type must be exported from the server and imported on the client; agents that place server code in the wrong bundle boundary will cause Next.js build errors or leak server logic to the client.
tRPC v10/v11 changed the router and procedure initialization API significantly from v9; agents trained on older tRPC patterns will generate initTRPC instead of the deprecated createRouter calls.
Protected procedures must be implemented as middleware (t.middleware) and composed with .use(); there is no built-in auth guard — agents must explicitly create one.
Subscriptions require a WebSocket link or splitLink configuration on the client; agents often wire up the default httpBatchLink which silently drops subscription calls.

Input schemas defined with Zod are validated at runtime but TypeScript inference only works when the Zod schema is passed directly to .input() — wrapping in a variable without inference annotation breaks client types.

security_score_components.security_notes: Context-based auth means security is entirely application-defined; agents must ensure protected procedures use middleware rather than ad-hoc ctx.session checks inside resolvers.
